content.text: A due diligence assessment that produces a signed-off risk register entry but leaves no lasting evidence trail is worthless at audit time. When a regulator, an internal audit team, or a data protection authority asks for evidence that a vendor was properly assessed before customer data was shared, the organisation needs a retrievable file: questionnaire responses, security certifications, DPA, scoring worksheet, and approval sign-offs. The evidence repository is the structured store that makes that evidence retrievable years after the original assessment was completed.
What Goes in the Evidence Repository

The repository holds one folder per vendor, versioned by assessment date. Each version folder contains a complete snapshot of the evidence as it existed at the time of the assessment — documents must not be retroactively modified, because the point of the archive is to show what the assessing team knew at the time.
Document
Required / Optional
Why it matters at audit time
Vendor questionnaire responses (PDF or exported JSON)
Required
Primary record of what the vendor represented about their security posture
SOC 2 Type II report or auditor confirmation letter
Required
Evidence that the vendor had external security validation at time of assessment
Penetration test executive summary
Required (if provided)
Evidence of the vendor's testing cadence and finding remediation
Data processing agreement (signed)
Required
Legal basis for processing personal data; demonstrates regulatory compliance
Contract or order form (signed)
Required
Establishes relationship scope; includes security addendum if negotiated
Risk register entry (at time of approval)
Required
Documents the risk score, accepted residual risks, and approver chain
Approval sign-offs (email chain or approval tool export)
Required
Demonstrates appropriate governance; critical for regulated industries
Sub-processor list (vendor-provided)
Required
Regulatory requirement under GDPR Article 28; needed for your own GDPR Article 30 records
Scoring worksheet
Required
Documents how the 1–5 domain scores were derived from the questionnaire responses
Incident notifications received
Conditional (if any occurred)
Demonstrates the vendor fulfilled their notification obligations
Annual attestation letters
Required (if agreed)
Ongoing confirmation of security posture between full reassessments
Repository Structure and Naming Conventions
Consistent naming and folder structure make it possible to find evidence quickly when needed — especially when the person conducting the audit is not the person who performed the original assessment.

# Repository root (SharePoint / Confluence / encrypted S3 bucket / GRC platform)
/vendors/
  anthropic/
    2025-09-15-initial-assessment/
      01-questionnaire-responses-2025-09-10.pdf
      02-soc2-type2-report-fy2025.pdf
      03-dpa-signed-2025-09-14.pdf
      04-contract-signed-2025-09-14.pdf
      05-sub-processor-list-2025-09-08.pdf
      06-scoring-worksheet-2025-09-15.xlsx
      07-risk-register-entry-2025-09-15.pdf
      08-approval-sign-offs-2025-09-15.pdf
    2026-09-01-annual-renewal/
      01-questionnaire-responses-2026-08-20.pdf
      02-soc2-type2-report-fy2026.pdf
      03-dpa-no-change-confirmed-2026-09-01.pdf
      04-scoring-worksheet-2026-09-01.xlsx
      05-risk-register-update-2026-09-01.pdf
      06-approval-sign-offs-2026-09-01.pdf
      07-score-delta-analysis.md        # NEW: compare scores to prior year

  contoso-ai-si/
    2026-06-15-initial-assessment/
      ...
    2026-07-14-incident-notification/   # TRIGGERED by incident
      01-vendor-notification-email.pdf
      02-internal-impact-assessment.pdf
      03-risk-register-update-2026-07-14.pdf
The
score-delta-analysis.md

file is added during annual renewals. It compares the current assessment scores to the prior year's scores and explains any changes. A score that dropped without explanation is a red flag in an audit.
Evidence Expiry and Automated Alerts

Security certifications and attestations have expiry dates. A SOC 2 Type II report more than 12 months old is considered stale by most enterprise security teams and regulators. Without automated alerts, expiry dates are silently missed and the organisation continues to rely on outdated evidence.

# repository/expiry_monitor.py
# Weekly job: check all vendor documents for upcoming expiry and alert owners.

import json
from datetime import date, timedelta
import httpx

CATALOG_PATH = "vendors/catalog.json"
SLACK_WEBHOOK = "<vault:security/slack-webhook-vendor-alerts>"
ALERT_THRESHOLDS_DAYS = [90, 60, 30, 14, 7]

def load_catalog() -> list[dict]:
    with open(CATALOG_PATH) as f:
        return json.load(f)

def send_alert(vendor: str, doc_type: str, expiry: date, days_left: int) -> None:
    severity = "🔴" if days_left <= 14 else "🟠" if days_left <= 30 else "🟡"
    message = (
        f"{severity} *Vendor evidence expiry alert*\n"
        f"Vendor: `{vendor}`\n"
        f"Document: `{doc_type}`\n"
        f"Expires: {expiry.isoformat()} ({days_left} days)\n"
        f"Action: Request updated document from vendor and file in repository.\n"
        f"Risk owner: <@{catalog_entry['risk_owner_slack_id']}>"
    )
    httpx.post(SLACK_WEBHOOK, json={"text": message})

def check_expiry():
    today = date.today()
    catalog = load_catalog()

    for vendor in catalog:
        for doc in vendor.get("tracked_documents", []):
            expiry = date.fromisoformat(doc["expiry_date"])
            days_left = (expiry - today).days

            if days_left in ALERT_THRESHOLDS_DAYS or (0 < days_left < 7):
                send_alert(vendor["name"], doc["type"], expiry, days_left)
            elif days_left <= 0:
                send_alert(vendor["name"], doc["type"] + " [EXPIRED]", expiry, 0)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    check_expiry()

The catalog.json file is updated whenever a new document is filed in the repository. Each entry tracks the document type, file path, collection date, and expiry date (if applicable). Documents without natural expiry dates — like signed contracts — are given a review date based on the contract term.
The Annual Renewal Process

Annual renewals are not a repeat of the full initial assessment. The goal is to confirm that the vendor's posture has not materially changed and to update any documents that have expired. A renewal takes significantly less effort than an initial assessment if the prior year's file is well-organised.
90 days before contract renewal.

The expiry monitor alerts the risk owner. The risk owner initiates the renewal by requesting updated documents from the vendor: current SOC 2 Type II report, updated questionnaire sections (data handling and security posture only, unless scope has changed), and updated sub-processor list.
60 days before renewal.

Documents received from vendor are filed in a new dated folder in the repository. The security team reviews the updated SOC 2 report for new exceptions and compares domain scores to the prior year. The score delta analysis is written.
If scores are unchanged or improved.
Risk register is updated with the new review date. Prior year's approval chain is noted; re-approval is only required if scope has changed or a score has dropped. Contract renewal proceeds.
If any score has dropped.

A gap analysis is written explaining the cause of the drop. If the drop is to 3, a remediation plan is required before contract renewal. If the drop is to 2, executive approval is required. If any score reaches 1, the contract renewal is paused and vendor migration is evaluated.
30 days before renewal.

All updated documents are in the repository. Risk register is updated. Legal confirms contract terms are still appropriate or negotiates updates. Approval sign-offs obtained. Contract renewal executed.
Triggered Renewals (Between Annual Cycles)
Some events require an out-of-cycle review of a vendor's evidence file even if the annual renewal is not due. The risk register entry includes a list of triggers that require an immediate review:
Trigger
Required action
Timeline
Security incident notification from vendor
Review incident scope and impact; update risk register; assess whether compensating controls are needed; notify privacy team if personal data was involved
Within 5 business days of notification
Vendor acquisition or change of control
Review new owner's security posture; confirm no change to data handling commitments; invoke change-in-control clause if new owner's terms are less protective
Within 30 days of public announcement
Material sub-processor change
Assess new sub-processor's security and compliance; update DPA if required; confirm data flow mapping remains accurate
Before the change takes effect (or within 5 days if vendor gives short notice)
New regulatory requirement affecting the vendor relationship
Assess whether existing DPA and contract terms cover the new requirement; negotiate updates if not; confirm vendor compliance timeline
Within 30 days of regulatory effective date
Scope expansion (your organisation starts sending a new data category)
Treat as a partial new assessment: re-evaluate data handling domain; update DPA to include new data type; re-obtain security sign-off
Before the new data type is first sent to the vendor
Repository Access and Retention

The evidence repository contains confidential information — vendor security reports, signed contracts, and internal risk assessments — and must be access-controlled. At the same time, it must be accessible to the people who need it: security team, legal, procurement, and auditors.
Access control: role-based; security team, legal, and procurement have read/write access; internal audit has read-only; other employees have no access.
Immutability: documents filed in the repository cannot be modified or deleted by end users. Only a repository admin can remove documents, and all removals are logged with justification.
Retention: 7 years minimum from the date of contract termination, to cover regulatory audit periods and statute of limitations for data protection violations.
Backup: repository is backed up daily to a separate location; recovery time objective (RTO) for the repository is 4 hours.
Auditor access: external auditors and regulators may be granted temporary read-only access to specific vendor folders without granting access to the full repository.

A single SharePoint folder with inconsistent naming, no expiry tracking, and no access controls is not an evidence repository — it is a document graveyard. The repository must be a system, not a location.
GRC Platform Integration

Larger organisations operate Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC) platforms — tools like ServiceNow GRC, OneTrust, Vanta, Drata, or in-house TPRM systems — that centralise third-party risk management across all vendor categories. Integrating LLM vendor assessments into the GRC platform ensures that AI-specific vendor risk is visible alongside conventional IT vendor risk in the same executive dashboards and audit reports.
What to Configure in the GRC Platform
Vendor record.
Create a vendor record for each LLM vendor or partner. Tag it with a custom attribute such as
vendor_type: llm_provider
or
vendor_type: ai_si
. This enables filtering all AI vendor risks in reporting.
Assessment template.

Create a custom assessment template with the five LLM-specific domains (data handling, security posture, compliance, reliability, exit/lock-in). Most GRC platforms support custom questionnaire templates that map responses to automated risk scores.
Document attachments.
Attach SOC 2 reports, DPAs, and other evidence directly to the vendor record with expiry dates. The platform tracks expiry and sends alerts automatically without a separate monitoring script.
Control mapping.

Map vendor assessment scores to your organisation's control framework (e.g., ISO 27001, NIST CSF, SOC 2 Trust Service Criteria). This translates the vendor risk score into language that compliance teams and auditors already understand.
Risk acceptance workflow.

GRC platforms support approval workflows. Configure the approval routing for each vendor criticality tier: Medium → security team; High → security + legal; Critical → security + legal + executive sponsor. Approvals are recorded immutably in the platform.
Where a File System Repository Still Makes Sense

GRC platforms handle structured data well but are poor at storing large binary files like full SOC 2 reports (often 100–300 pages). A hybrid approach works: the GRC platform holds the structured assessment (scores, risk register entries, workflow approvals, expiry alerts) while a secure file store (encrypted S3, SharePoint with access controls) holds the raw documents, linked by URL from the GRC record.
Using the Repository During an Audit

When an external auditor or data protection authority requests evidence of vendor due diligence, the repository must support rapid, selective disclosure. The following workflow applies to both internal audit reviews and external regulatory investigations.
Scope the request.

Identify which vendors are in scope for the audit. For a GDPR audit, all vendors who process EU personal data are in scope — use the sub-processor register (derived from the repository) to compile this list quickly.
Compile the evidence package.

For each in-scope vendor, assemble: the most recent complete assessment folder, plus any triggered review folders since the last annual renewal. The package demonstrates continuous due diligence, not just a one-time gate.
Redact if necessary.

SOC 2 reports and penetration test summaries contain sensitive security information. Before providing them to external parties, confirm whether the vendor's confidentiality obligations require redaction of certain sections. Many SOC 2 reports are provided under NDA.
Demonstrate the process, not just the evidence.

Auditors often want to understand how the due diligence process works, not just see the output. The scoring worksheet, the approval chain, and the renewal calendar together demonstrate that the organisation has a repeatable process — not just a document archive.
Document the disclosure.
Record in the repository that the evidence package was disclosed to [auditor/authority] on [date], for [purpose]. This creates an audit trail of who has seen the sensitive vendor security documents.
Maintaining the Sub-Processor Register (GDPR Article 30)

GDPR Article 30 requires data controllers and processors to maintain a record of processing activities. For enterprises using LLM vendors, this record must include every sub-processor who handles personal data — which means every LLM vendor, reseller, or partner whose service processes prompts that may contain personal data. The evidence repository's sub-processor documents are the input to this mandatory register.

# gdpr/sub_processor_register.py
# Generate GDPR Article 30 records of processing from the vendor catalog.
# Each entry describes one processing activity involving a LLM sub-processor.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class SubProcessorRecord:
    # Article 30(1)(a): controller name and contact
    controller_name: str
    dpo_contact: str

    # Article 30(1)(b): purposes of processing
    processing_purpose: str          # e.g. "Contract analysis via LLM-assisted extraction"

    # Article 30(1)(c): categories of data subjects
    data_subject_categories: list[str]   # e.g. ["contract counterparties", "employees"]

    # Article 30(1)(d): categories of personal data
    personal_data_categories: list[str]  # e.g. ["names", "email addresses", "signatures"]

    # Article 30(1)(e): recipients / sub-processors
    sub_processor_name: str
    sub_processor_country: str
    sub_processor_contract_ref: str  # DPA reference in evidence repository

    # Article 30(1)(f): third country transfers
    transfer_to_third_country: bool
    transfer_safeguard: Optional[str]   # e.g. "SCCs (EU Commission Decision 2021/914)"

    # Article 30(1)(g): retention / deletion
    retention_period: str            # e.g. "30 days (prompt logs); 7 years (audit trail)"

    # Internal metadata (not required by Article 30, but useful)
    assessment_file: str             # path to evidence repository folder
    last_reviewed: date
    next_review: date

# Example entry for Anthropic as LLM processor
example = SubProcessorRecord(
    controller_name="Acme Corp",
    dpo_contact="dpo@acmecorp.example",
    processing_purpose="Legal document analysis — clause extraction and summarisation",
    data_subject_categories=["contract counterparties", "employee signatories"],
    personal_data_categories=["names", "job titles", "email addresses", "signatures"],
    sub_processor_name="Anthropic, PBC",
    sub_processor_country="United States",
    sub_processor_contract_ref="vendors/anthropic/2026-09-01-annual-renewal/03-dpa-signed-2026-09-01.pdf",
    transfer_to_third_country=True,
    transfer_safeguard="Standard Contractual Clauses (EU Commission Decision 2021/914)",
    retention_period="Zero prompt retention (confirmed in DPA clause 4.2)",
    assessment_file="vendors/anthropic/2026-09-01-annual-renewal/",
    last_reviewed=date(2026, 9, 1),
    next_review=date(2027, 9, 1),
)

The register must be maintained in writing (which includes electronic form) and made available to the supervisory authority on request. It should be updated whenever a new LLM vendor is added, a vendor is removed, the scope of processing changes, or the vendor's sub-processor list changes. The evidence repository's catalog.json is the operational source of truth that feeds the Article 30 register.
Common Pitfalls in Evidence Management
Pitfall
What goes wrong
Prevention
Filing evidence after the fact
Assessment is completed and vendor approved, but documents are filed weeks later or not at all. If an audit occurs before filing, there is no evidence trail.
File evidence within 5 business days of receipt; make repository filing a mandatory step in the approval workflow before the approval is considered complete
Stale SOC 2 as "good enough"
A vendor provides the prior year's SOC 2 report, which is 14 months old. The assessment team accepts it to avoid delay. A data breach 3 months later reveals controls had lapsed.
Enforce the 12-month freshness rule; request the current report or an auditor bridge letter; do not accept reports older than 12 months without documented justification
Questionnaire responses not saved
A vendor answers a security questionnaire via email; the email thread is the only record. The employee who conducted the assessment leaves; the evidence is lost.
Require vendors to submit questionnaire responses in a structured format (PDF or web form); convert email answers to a PDF and file immediately
Single point of knowledge
Only one person knows where the evidence is, how to retrieve it, and what it means. When they go on leave during an audit, the response is delayed.
Repository structure and catalog must be self-documenting; train at least two people per vendor relationship; use the GRC platform for structured data, not personal knowledge
Scope creep without re-assessment
A vendor originally assessed to handle anonymised telemetry is later used to process identifiable user data. No re-assessment is triggered.
Scope expansion must be flagged in the change management process and trigger a partial re-assessment before the expanded data type is shared; the catalog entry tracks the approved data categories
Treating renewal as a rubber stamp
Annual renewal is conducted but scores are not compared to prior year; a domain that dropped from 4 to 2 is not caught until an incident occurs.
Require the score delta analysis document for every renewal; any drop requires an explanation and re-approval if the score crosses a tier boundary
Summary

The evidence repository is the operational backbone of a repeatable vendor due diligence programme. It stores a complete, immutable record of every assessment: questionnaire responses, security certifications, signed contracts, risk register entries, and approval chains. Consistent naming conventions and structured folder hierarchies make evidence retrievable years after the assessment. Automated expiry monitoring prevents the organisation from unknowingly relying on stale certifications. Annual renewals confirm that vendor posture has not materially changed without repeating the full initial assessment from scratch. Triggered out-of-cycle reviews respond to incidents, acquisitions, scope changes, and new regulatory requirements. Together, these practices transform due diligence from a one-time gate into a continuously maintained record of the organisation's vendor risk posture.
